Subject: Concerned about the flagging pattern — we need to discuss

Hi RestWell team,

I need to raise something because it's becoming a real problem. Over the past three weeks, I've submitted 9 sleep aids for safety review, and you've flagged or rejected 6 of them. That's a 67% flag rate. When I brought the same list to my psychiatrist — the one who prescribes both my sertraline and my alprazolam and knows my complete medication profile — she cleared 7 of the 9 and had concerns about 2.

Let me give you some specific examples:
- You flagged magnesium glycinate at 400mg because of "potential additive sedative effects with benzodiazepines." My psychiatrist prescribes alprazolam to dozens of patients who take magnesium. She said this is "not a clinically meaningful interaction at supplemental doses" and that she actively recommends magnesium to patients on benzodiazepines for sleep support.
- You rejected chamomile tea extract because of "potential CYP3A4 interaction with alprazolam." I showed this to my pharmacist and he actually sighed. He said chamomile has trace enzyme activity that's never been shown to affect alprazolam levels at the dose in a standard supplement. He drinks chamomile tea himself before bed.
- You flagged L-theanine because of "serotonergic activity that could interact with sertraline." L-theanine is literally found in green tea. I drink green tea every morning and my sertraline works fine. You're telling me I can't take in supplement form what I already consume in my tea.

The practical impact of all this flagging: I've now been dealing with insomnia for over three months with no OTC sleep aid support because every product I try to review gets flagged. My sleep has deteriorated to the point where I'm averaging 4-5 hours a night. My therapist said last week that my anxiety scores have gotten worse since I started using RestWell — not because of the app itself, but because the constant rejections have left me with no sleep solutions. I've started taking my alprazolam more frequently (4-5 times a week instead of 2-3) because it's the only thing that helps me sleep, which is exactly the opposite of what my psychiatrist wants.

I've also spent about $280 on extra appointments — two psychiatrist copays and a pharmacist consultation — to verify your safety warnings. Every provider said the same thing: the mainstream OTC products I was asking about are safe at standard doses for someone on my medications.

I'll be direct: if the next round of reviews follows the same pattern — flagging standard OTC sleep supplements that my own providers have no concerns about — I'm going to cancel RestWell and rely on my psychiatrist's recommendations directly.

Karen

---

Subject: Talked to someone about this — comparing notes

Hi RestWell team,

Following up on my earlier email. My partner mentioned the flagging issue to his colleague Sarah, who takes escitalopram (same drug class as my sertraline — both SSRIs) and also uses an as-needed benzodiazepine. Sarah uses a different sleep app called DreamGuard and she pulled up her review history for comparison.

Out of 12 sleep products she submitted last quarter, DreamGuard approved 9, flagged 2 (a specific herbal with documented CYP3A4 inhibition at supplemental doses, and a product containing phenibut which has real GABA receptor agonist activity), and recommended a dose adjustment on 1. That's a 75% approval rate. RestWell flagged or rejected 67% of my submissions — nearly the inverse of what Sarah's app did.

The products DreamGuard approved for Sarah included magnesium glycinate, L-theanine, tart cherry, and chamomile — all of which RestWell flagged for me. The ones DreamGuard flagged were products with genuine pharmacological activity at the doses in question. Sarah said she trusts DreamGuard because its flags always correspond to things her pharmacist would also flag.

Sarah made a point that really resonated: she said the purpose of a safety review tool is to give you confidence, not to make you afraid. When an app flags cherry juice and chamomile tea alongside products that have real drug interactions, you lose the ability to tell which warnings matter. And then you either take everything (ignoring all warnings) or take nothing (following all warnings). Neither of those is good health management. Good safety screening helps you take the RIGHT things with confidence.

I want RestWell to work. I desperately need better sleep. But I need reviews that are calibrated to actual clinical risk, not theoretical worst-case scenarios. If this doesn't improve, I'll switch to DreamGuard or just work directly with my psychiatrist on sleep aid selection.

Karen
